comments.author: @Paraserima
comments.text:
As a Korean, I find it a bit cringeworthy when people glorify Korea with excessive nationalism. We weren't trying to become some inspirational success story. We were simply trying to escape poverty. Having experienced poverty ourselves, we know that it is never something to romanticize.

Historically, Korea was never a great power, but it was not an especially poor country either. It was only in the 19th century that an **8-year-old king, an 11-year-old king, and a 19-year-old king (a person who had spent his life as a farmer on a small island)** became puppet kings. In reality, powerful aristocratic families controlled the country and spent the nation's tax revenue on parties and their own lavish lifestyles. That was when Korea began to decline.

When the national treasury was exhausted, those aristocratic families started selling anything they could in order to raise new taxes to fund their extravagant lifestyles. At the time, if you had enough money, you could literally buy a government position. For example, you could become a mayor simply by paying for the office. Then, to recover the money they had invested in buying those positions, many officials began exploiting ordinary people who had little or no money.

As the country grew weaker, Korea eventually fell under Japanese colonial rule and later suffered through the Korean War. By the end of it all, we had nothing left.
From that point on, Koreans united with one goal: to escape poverty. The entire nation worked relentlessly.

When the founder of POSCO started the company with its initial investment, he said:

> "This money is no different from money paid for with the blood of our ancestors. If this business venture fails, we should throw ourselves into the sea and die."


I often see people from developing countries say they want Korea's economic development but not its fierce competition. Honestly, I find that attitude ridiculous. It's like saying you want an engine to run but don't want it to produce heat.

Personally, I wanted to become wealthy, so I looked up many interviews with rich and successful people. Every single one of them had one thing in common: they didn't pursue work-life balance. They worked like crazy. Koreans did the same during the country's industrialization. Many factory workers worked 13-hour days.

When wealthy people say that they were simply lucky to succeed, it is often an expression of humility. Before they became successful, they worked relentlessly. It's just that most people never saw that part of their journey.

Until about 25 years ago, Koreans had only one day off each week—Sunday. Elon Musk and Bill Gates also worked obsessively before they became successful.

Warren Buffett has often said that he was fortunate to be born into a well-off white American family. However, few people know that his success also came from an extraordinary level of dedication. He was so obsessed with investing that he read financial statements even while sitting in parks, and over the course of about 70 years, he read and memorized more than 100,000 financial statements.

You don't have to admire wealthy people. But if your goal is to become wealthy yourself, you should at least respect their advice.

I often see people dismiss successful individuals by saying they were simply lucky. Then they claim that, although they are poor, they are enjoying life and are therefore better off than those rich people. Most of them will probably never become wealthy.

Becoming wealthy is not easy.
Just choose one.

comments.text: There is a lot of talk about Korea becoming a rich country in decades from the poorest country on the planet, which is poorer than Africa. However, Korea is a rare country that has maintained a centralized state for 3,000 years, and it is unheard of even in the world where education and culture flourished thousands of years ago. It had been a country that used ceramics for a thousand years already, and Japan kidnapped a Korean ceramic craftsman about 300 years ago and managed to make ceramics. The world's first metal type was made and used, and its publication is where it exists. It is the place where the world's first rain gauge was invented and has been observing celestial bodies since 2000 years ago. Although there have been difficulties in the history of the past 100 years or so, the passion for education, culture, and dedication to the family have rejuvenated them.

As a Korean, I’d like to express my deep gratitude for creating such a beautiful and spiritual video.
I wanted to add a bit more context about the Emille Bell from the Silla Dynasty, as the story can sometimes be misunderstood. The tale that a child was melted down to cast the bell appears to be a form of oral legend. Given that it was the golden age of Buddhism in Korea, it’s best understood as a metaphorical story rather than a literal one. Buddhism emphasizes transcendence beyond the physical body, and this legend seems to have emerged from those profound philosophical roots.
The Silla period was a truly magnificent and beautiful era across many fields, and historical evidence clearly shows it was a time filled with humanity and compassion.
Thank you once again for this wonderful video. I sincerely wish your channel continued success and prosperity!

comments.author: @koreanpronunciationmaster
comments.text:
Korean History the World Rarely Knows
The Mongols conquered the entire world. Yet, Kublai Khan, the Emperor of the Mongol Empire, personally made a promise known as 'Seojo-guje (世祖舊制)' (the Old Systems of the Founder), stating, "We will recognize and maintain the customs, national system, and traditions of Goryeo as they are." In the entire world, Goryeo was the only nation to receive such exceptional treatment from the Mongol Empire. The ruling class and historians of the Mongol Empire viewed Goryeo and Goguryeo as essentially an extension of the same state. They vividly remembered the historical fact that Goguryeo had been a military superpower in Northeast Asia. Later, a Goryeo woman even rose to the position of Empress of the Yuan Dynasty. While China was fully conquered and suffered a loss of its culture and history, Goryeo successfully preserved its long-standing culture and historical identity.

The Korean Wave (Hallyu) in the Mongol Empire
The Mongols and Chinese, who did not traditionally enjoy eating fresh vegetables, began adopting the Goryeo practice of Sangchu-ssam (lettuce wraps). Up until the early Ming Dynasty, this culture gained massive popularity among the Chinese and Mongol aristocracy under the names 'Goryeo-po' or 'Goryeo-ssam.' Furthermore, Goryeo's traditional attire—featuring the style of a voluminous skirt paired with a jeogori (jacket)—became a major trend among Chinese women during the Yuan and Ming Dynasties, known as Goryeoyang (Goryeo style). The connection continues today: even now, Korean convenience stores thrive in the country, with CU and GS25 operating 441 and 267 stores in Mongolia, respectively.

세계가 잘 모르는 한국의 역사
전세계를 정복한 몽골. 몽골의 황제 쿠빌라이 칸이 직접 내린 이 약속 "고려의 풍습과 국체, 전통을 있는 그대로 인정하고 유지하겠다"는 내용의 '세조구제(世祖舊制)’를 약속. 전 세계를 통틀어 몽골 제국으로부터 이처럼 파격적인 대우를 받은 나라는 고려 뿐이었습니다. 몽골 제국의 지배층과 역사가들은 고려와 고구려를 사실상 같은 국가의 연장선으로 파악했습니다. 고구려가 동북아시아의 군사 강국이었다는 역사적 사실을 매우 강렬하게 기억하고 있었습니다. 이후 고려의 여인이 원나라의 황후 자리에 오르기도 했습니다. 중국은 완전히 정복당해 그들의 문화와 역사를 잃어버린 반면, 고려는 오랜 문화와 역사적 정체성을 성공적으로 지켜냈습니다.

몽골의 한류: 채소를 즐겨 먹지 않던 몽골인과 중국인들이 고려인들의 상추쌈 문화를 따라 먹기 시작했으며, 명나라 초기까지 '고려포(高麗包)'나 '고려쌈'이라는 이름으로 중국과 몽골 귀족 사회에 큰 인기를 끌었습니다. 고려의 한복, 풍성한 치마와 저고리 조합의 스타일이 원~명대 중국 여인들 사이에서 고려양으로 유행함.

replies.author: @koreanpronunciationmaster
replies.text:
The Ban on Goryeoyang, Goryeo's Hanbok Style: The Original 'Gangnam Style'

From ancient times, Jeju Island possessed exceptionally advanced skills in processing horsehair. Traditional headwear worn by Joseon men, such as the gat (brimmed hat), manggeon (headband), and tanggeon (inner cap), were all manufactured based on Jeju's horsehair craftsmanship.

Mami-gun (馬尾裙), a Joseon-style voluminous petticoat woven from horsehair, was introduced to China through direct maritime trade between Jeju Island and the Jiangnan region (including Suzhou and Shanghai). Initially debuting in southern China, Mami-gun sparked a massive "Gangnam Style" craze that swept through the entire Ming Dynasty.

As demand skyrocketed for horsehair—the essential material needed to make Mami-gun—supplies fell drastically short. This led to a bizarre crisis where people began secretly cutting off the tails and manes of government-owned horses and military steeds. The rampant theft created severe complications for national military horse management and disrupted social discipline. Consequently, in 1498, Hongzhi, the 11th emperor of the Ming Dynasty, imposed a total ban on Goryeoyang (Goryeo-style Hanbok).

According to records in the Shuyuan Zaji (菽園雜記), a collection of essays written by Ming official Lu Rong, the Joseon-style horsehair petticoat was an absolute sensation, spreading on such a massive scale that it would not be an exaggeration to say Ming society was completely "obsessed" with it.

고려의 한복, 고려양 금지. 원조 ‘강남 스타일’

제주도는 예로부터 말총을 다루는 기술이 매우 뛰어났습니다. 조선 남성들이 쓰던 갓(총모자), 망건, 탕건 등도 모두 제주의 말총 공예를 기반으로 제작되었습니다.  말총으로 만든 조선식 풍성한 속치마인 마미군(馬尾裙)은 제주도와 중국 강남(소주, 상하이 등) 지역의 직접적인 해상 교류를 통해 전파되었음. 마미군이 중국 남부 지방에 먼저 소개되었고, 이것이 명나라 전체를 뒤흔든 '강남 스타일' 유행으로 번졌는데. 마미군을 만드는 데 필수적인 '말총' 수요가 폭발하자, 이를 구하지 못한 사람들이 관청의 말 꼬리와 갈기까지 몰래 잘라가는 사태가 벌어졌습니다. 이로 인해 국가의 역마(군마) 관리와 사회 기강에 큰 문제가 발생했고. 명나라 제11대 황제인 홍치제(1498년)가 고려양 (고려 한복 스타일)을 전면 금지함. 명나라 관리 육용(陸容)이 저술한 시문집 《숙원잡기(菽園雜記)》의 기록을 보면, 당시 명나라 사회가 조선식 속치마(마미군)에 "미쳐 있었다"고 표현해도 과언이 아닐 만큼 엄청난 규모로 유행했다고 기록함.

comments.author: @yonggheelee1743
comments.text:
I would like to speak about Korea's underlying strength from a different perspective. During the Joseon Dynasty, Korea had a social class system, and the upper class was the Yangban. They comprised 10% of the total population. These individuals were philosophers who studied for over ten years to become civil servants. They exchanged opinions on philosophical issues with their teachers and friends. In other words, it was a country where 10% of the population were philosophers. There were schools in every town, and society placed the utmost importance on scholarship. Korea is a nation with such underlying strength. In modern terms, the upper class were all philosophers who valued learning above all else. Although Korea lagged behind in the development of natural science, suffering colonial rule by Japan—which adopted Western science early—and subsequently enduring the Korean War, the tradition of revering scholarship continues to this day. Korean students study an enormous amount; speaking from my experience of both American and Korean education systems, if Korean and American students take a math exam using the same test paper, the Korean student submits their answer sheet and leaves the classroom while the American student still hasn't fully grasped the questions. This is not because Korean students are naturally gifted, but because they have been trained. 
American students can get good grades simply by solving problems from their school math textbooks, consider themselves good at math, and use those scores to get into prestigious universities. But in Korea, that level of skill would only amount to a 40 out of 100. Korean students have to solve 25 problems in 50 minutes, 5 of which are essay questions. They feel regretful if they get even a few questions wrong on such an exam, so they buy and solve high-difficulty workbooks with the goal of getting a perfect score on the next test. Therefore, when American students see Korean students solving difficult math problems incredibly quickly using only their brains without a calculator, they feel like they are an insurmountable wall. For reference, the math curriculum in the U.S. lags behind Korea by about 2 to 3 years. So, in terms of math and science alone, Korean students have effectively attended one more school than American students. Having experienced both countries’ education systems and taught gifted students at one of Korea’s science high schools for gifted students, where students could mentally multiply three-digit numbers, I believe that the top 30% of students at schools in major Korean cities can succeed at prestigious universities such as Harvard, Yale, MIT, Caltech, Berkeley, and Stanford. If fair competition were allowed without any restrictions, the most prestigious universities in the United States would be filled with Korean students.
